  $M_{b_2}$ depends only on $\tau'$ and $\lambda_2$.
  The doubly-dynamical system~\eqref{eq:ddybe}
  decouples into two independent copies of the genus-$1$
  DYBE~\eqref{eq:dybe-g1}, one on each handle.
\item The ordered chiral homology satisfies a
  factorisation constraint at this boundary stratum of
  $\overline{\cM}_2$:
  \begin{equation}\label{eq:sep-factorisation}
    \int_{\Sigma_2}^{\mathrm{ord}} Y_\hbar
    \;\xrightarrow{\;\mathrm{res}\;}\;
    \bigoplus_{\mu = 0}^{k}\,
    \int_{E_\tau, p}^{\mathrm{ord}}
    Y_\hbar \otimes V_\mu
    \;\otimes\;
    \int_{E_{\tau'}, p}^{\mathrm{ord}}
    Y_\hbar \otimes V_\mu^*,
  \end{equation}
  where the sum runs over the integrable representations
  $V_0, V_1, \ldots, V_k$ of
  $\widehat{\mathfrak{sl}}_2$ at level $k$
  \textup{(}the $k + 1$ fusion channels at the
  node\textup{)}, and
  $V_\mu^*$ is the contragredient.
\item The Euler characteristic decomposes through the
  Verlinde fusion rules, \emph{not} as a naive product.
  The degree-$2$ Euler characteristic
  $\chi = -12$ on $\Sigma_2 \setminus \{0\}$
  decomposes as
  \begin{equation}\label{eq:euler-sep-decomp}
    \chi(\Sigma_2 \setminus \{0\},\,\cL_{\mathrm{KZB}})
    = \sum_{\mu=0}^{k} \dim(V_\mu)\,
    \chi(E_\tau \setminus \{p\},\,
    \cL_\mu)
    \cdot
    \chi(E_{\tau'} \setminus \{p\},\,
    \cL_{\mu^*}),
  \end{equation}
  where $\cL_\mu$ is the KZB local system on the
  punctured elliptic curve with the $V_\mu$-channel
  inserted at the node.
  Each factor
  $\chi(E_\tau \setminus \{p\}, \cL_\mu)
  = -\dim(V_\mu) \cdot d$
  is the Euler characteristic of a rank-$\dim(V_\mu)
  \cdot d$ local system on a once-punctured torus
  \textup{(}$\chi(E_\tau \setminus \{p\}) = -1$\textup{)}.
  The factorisation through fusion channels means the
  decomposition is \emph{not} simply
  $(-4) \cdot (-4) = 16$
  \textup{(}which gives the wrong sign and magnitude\textup{)};
  the correct answer accounts for
  the rank enhancement and the Verlinde multiplicities
  at the node.
\end{enumerate}
\end{proposition}

\begin{proof}
(i)~At $\Omega_{12} = 0$, the period matrix is
block-diagonal and the holomorphic differentials
$\omega_1, \omega_2$ localise to the two components:
$\omega_1$ is supported on $E_\tau$ and
$\omega_2$ on $E_{\tau'}$.
The quasi-periodicity~\eqref{eq:szego-quasiperiod}
decouples: $S_2(z + B_1, w) = S_2(z,w) -
2\pi i\,\omega_1(z)$ involves only the $E_\tau$-period,
and similarly for $B_2$.
The $B$-cycle monodromies
$M_{b_\alpha}
= \exp(-2\pi i\,\hbar\,\Omega \cdot \omega_\alpha)$
become independent because the off-diagonal coupling
through $\Omega_{12}$ vanishes.

(ii)~The factorisation is the standard sewing
constraint for chiral homology at a separating node
(Beilinson--Drinfeld~\cite{BD04}): the chiral homology
of the nodal curve is the sum over integrable
representations at the given level
of the tensor product of the chiral homologies of the
two components, with the fusion channel $V_\mu$
inserted at the node on one side and $V_\mu^*$ on the
other. For $\widehat{\mathfrak{sl}}_2$ at level~$k$,
the integrable modules $V_0, \ldots, V_k$ span the
fusion category.

(iii)~On a once-punctured torus with a rank-$r$ local
system, $\chi = r \cdot (2 - 2 - 1) = -r$. With
$V_\mu$ inserted at the node, the local system
on each $E \setminus \{p\}$ has rank
$\dim(V_\mu) \cdot d$, giving
$\chi = -\dim(V_\mu) \cdot d$. The
factorisation~\eqref{eq:euler-sep-decomp} sums these
products over $\mu$, weighted by the channel
multiplicity $\dim(V_\mu)$ from the trace over
the fusion space at the node. The naive product
$(-4)(-4) = 16$ fails on two counts: it ignores the
sum over fusion channels, and it miscomputes the sign
(each factor is negative, their product positive, but
the total $\chi = -12$ is negative).
\end{proof}

\begin{remark}[The separating degeneration contains no
genuinely genus-$2$ information]
\label{rem:sep-no-new-info}
\index{degeneration!separating contains no new data}
The separating degeneration
$\Sigma_2 \to E_\tau \cup E_{\tau'}$ is entirely
determined by genus-$1$ data: the factorisation
formula~\eqref{eq:sep-factorisation} expresses the
separating boundary contribution in terms of genus-$1$
ordered chiral homology and the fusion algebra.
Every coefficient in the separating channel is computable
from the genus-$1$ $R$-matrix eigenvalues (equivalently,
the $S$-matrix of the WZW model at level~$k$).
This is a general phenomenon: at any genus~$g$, the
separating degenerations are controlled by lower-genus
data through the fusion rules.
The genuinely new content at genus~$g$ comes from the
\emph{non-separating} degenerations, which carry
information about the full $g$-fold dynamical structure
that cannot be recovered from genera $< g$ by sewing.
\end{remark}

\begin{remark}[Genuinely genus-$2$ content: the
off-diagonal modular parameter]
\label{rem:genuinely-g2}
\index{genus-$2$!genuinely new content}
The new mathematical content at genus~$2$ that is
invisible at genus~$0$ and genus~$1$ is concentrated in
the off-diagonal period $\Omega_{12}$ and its
consequences:
\begin{enumerate}[label=\textup{(\alph*)}]
\item \emph{Three modular parameters versus one.}
  The genus-$1$ KZB system has a single modular
  parameter $d\tau$. The genus-$2$ system has three:
  $d\Omega_{11}$, $d\Omega_{12}$, $d\Omega_{22}$.
  The diagonal parameters $d\Omega_{11}$ and
  $d\Omega_{22}$ are genus-$1$-like (each controls one
  handle). The off-diagonal $d\Omega_{12}$ is
  genuinely genus-$2$: it measures the
  interaction between the two handles and has no
  lower-genus analogue.
\item \emph{Coupling of dynamical variables.}
  The heat equation~\eqref{eq:ddybe-coupling} at
  $(\alpha,\beta) = (1,2)$ gives
  $\partial_{\Omega_{12}} R
  = (2\pi i)^{-1}\,
  \partial_{\lambda_1}\partial_{\lambda_2} R$:
  the off-diagonal modular flow mixes the two dynamical
  variables.
  This mixed derivative has no genus-$1$ counterpart.
  It controls the rate at which the two dynamical
  $R$-matrices (one per handle) fail to commute.
\item \emph{Non-separating degeneration data.}
  In the non-separating degeneration
  $\Sigma_2 \to E_\tau$
  (Proposition~\ref{prop:g2-nonsep-degen}),
  the limit $\epsilon \to 0$ sends $\Omega_{12}
  = \epsilon \to 0$, but the \emph{rate} of approach
  carries a period integral from the pinched cycle
  that encodes the genus-$2$ sewing parameter.
  This parameter is the plumbing fixture
  $q_{\mathrm{sew}} = e^{2\pi i\,\Omega_{22}}$;
  the coefficient of
  $q_{\mathrm{sew}}^n$ in the ordered chiral
  homology produces the $n$-th sewing correction, which
  is invisible to the genus-$1$ theory.
\item \emph{Euler characteristic growth.}
  At genus~$1$, $\chi(\Conf_n^{\mathrm{ord}}(E_\tau))
  = 0$ for all $n \geq 1$: the Euler characteristic
  carries no information.
  At genus~$2$,
  $\chi(\Conf_n^{\mathrm{ord}}(\Sigma_2))
  = \prod_{j=0}^{n-1}(-2-j) \neq 0$
  (Proposition~\ref{prop:g2-euler-n}), providing
  genuine lower bounds on cohomology dimensions at
  every degree. The growth
  $|\chi| \sim (n+1)!$ reflects the increasing
  complexity of the genus-$2$ surface braid group.
\item \emph{Matrix-valued Hodge data.}
  The $(\mathrm{Im}\,\Omega)^{-1}$ matrix entering the
  de~Rham harmonic projection
  (equation~\eqref{eq:im-omega-inv}) is a genuine
  $2 \times 2$ matrix at genus~$2$, not the scalar
  $1/\mathrm{Im}(\tau)$ of genus~$1$.
  The off-diagonal entry
  $-\mathrm{Im}(\Omega_{12})/
  \det(\mathrm{Im}\,\Omega)$ couples the two
  holomorphic differentials in the harmonic
  projection and produces cross-terms in the genus-$2$
  propagator that are invisible to genus-$1$ sewing.
\end{enumerate}
In summary: genus~$0$ sees conformal blocks (KZ
equation, rational $R$-matrix). Genus~$1$ adds the
quantum group parameter $q = e^{2\pi i\,\hbar}$ through
the single $B$-cycle (KZB equation, elliptic $R$-matrix,
DYBE). Genus~$2$ adds the doubly-dynamical coupling
through $\Omega_{12}$: the two quantum group parameters
interact, and the interaction is governed by the
off-diagonal heat equation. This three-step escalation
(rational $\to$ elliptic $\to$
Siegel-modular) is the genus hierarchy of the
ordered chiral homology programme.
\end{remark}


% ================================================================
% MOVED FROM between ``Examples'' and ``Chiral quantum groups''; now after elliptic curves
\section{Comparison with related work}
\label{sec:comparison}

The ordered chiral homology calculation above should be read as the
genus-$2$ continuation of the genus-$0$ KZ and genus-$1$ KZB stories.
The new datum is the off-diagonal period $\Omega_{12}$: it couples the
two dynamical variables, produces the mixed heat equation, and records
the genuinely genus-$2$ transport that is absent from lower genus.

What follows extracts the algebraic shadow of that transport. Rather
than repeating the lower-genus comparison, we pass to the spectral-cover
package in which branch data and defect transport are visible on the
ordered side before averaging to scalar invariants.
%% ================================================================
%% SPECTRAL COVERS AND THE DEFECT CALCULUS
%% ================================================================

\section{Spectral covers and the defect calculus}
\label{sec:spectral-covers-defect}
\index{spectral cover|textbf}
\index{defect calculus!tensor}
\index{lifted spectral presentation}

The bar-cohomology generating function $P_{\cA^!}(x)$ is a visible
shadow of a richer object: a finite cover $\pi \colon \Sigma \to X$
of the spectral line $X = \mathbf{P}^1_x$ whose sheets correspond to
the eigenvalues of the Kodaira--Spencer operator
$\mathrm{KS}_\cA$. The spectral discriminant~$\Delta_\cA(x)$
is the branch locus of this cover. Transport between algebras (via
Drinfeld--Sokolov reduction, tensor product, level-rank duality) lives
not on the visible shadow but on the lifted cover itself. In the
quadratic regime (two sheets), transport is governed by a common-sheet
law; in the cubic regime (three sheets), by resolvent towers and
Kummer theory. The resulting calculus produces a strict
hierarchy of invariants, each invisible to its predecessors.

Throughout, $k$ is algebraically closed of characteristic~$0$;
$F := k(x)$, $X := \mathbf{P}^1_x$.

\subsection{Spectral presentations and branch packages}
\label{subsec:spectral-presentations}

\begin{definition}[Spectral presentation]
\label{def:spectral-presentation}
\index{spectral presentation|textbf}
Let $P(x)$ be a visible spectral series. A \emph{spectral
presentation} of~$P$ consists of a finite separable extension~$L/F$,
a primitive element $y \in L$ with $L = F(y)$, and a rational
reconstruction law $r(x,t) \in F(t)$ such that $P(x) = r(x,y)$.
The presentation is \emph{tautological} if $y = P$ and $r(x,t) = t$;
it is \emph{hidden} if $P \in F$ but $L \neq F$.
\end{definition}

Hidden presentations are the key phenomenon: the same rational visible
series~$P(x)$ may arise as a rational expression in a nontrivial
quadratic or cubic coordinate on a finite cover. The tautological
field $F(P) = F$ forgets the cover entirely; the lifted presentation
remembers it.

\begin{construction}[Lifted spectral cover package; \ClaimStatusProvedHere]
\label{cons:lifted-spectral-package}
\index{spectral cover!lifted package}
From a spectral presentation $(L, y, r)$ with normalized cover
$\pi \colon \Sigma \to X$, extract:
\begin{enumerate}[label=\textup{(\roman*)}]
\item The \emph{sheet field} $L = F(y)$ and the $F$-vector
 space $E := L$.
\item The \emph{sheet operator}
 $M_y \in \operatorname{End}_F(E)$: multiplication by~$y$.
 Its minimal polynomial~$m_y(T) \in F[T]$ has degree
 $[L:F]$ and equals the characteristic polynomial.
\item The \emph{branch complex}
 $\mathbb{B}(\Sigma/X) := L_{\Sigma/X}$
 (the cotangent complex of the cover).
\item The \emph{branch space}
 $V^{\mathrm{br}}(\Sigma/X)
 := H^0(\Sigma, \Omega^1_{\Sigma/X})$,
 a finite-dimensional torsion sheaf supported on the
 ramification locus.
\item If~$\pi$ is unramified over $x = 0$, the
 \emph{branch operator}
 $T^{\mathrm{br}}_{\Sigma/X}
 := \pi^*(1/x) \cdot \mathrm{id}$.
\end{enumerate}
\end{construction}

\begin{theorem}[Determinantal branch formula; \ClaimStatusProvedHere]
\label{thm:determinantal-branch-formula}
\index{branch formula!determinantal}
Assume~$\pi$ is unramified over $x = 0$. Let
$\operatorname{Supp}\Omega^1_{\Sigma/X}
= \{p_1, \ldots, p_N\}$ with
$\ell_i :=
\operatorname{length}_{\cO_{\Sigma,p_i}}
\Omega^1_{\Sigma/X,p_i}$. Then
\[
\det\bigl(1 - z\, T^{\mathrm{br}}_{\Sigma/X}\bigr)
= \prod_{i=1}^{N}
 \left(1 - \frac{z}{x(\pi(p_i))}\right)^{\ell_i}.
\]
The branch operator records the branch divisor with full
scheme-theoretic multiplicities.
\end{theorem}

\begin{proof}
Decompose $V^{\mathrm{br}} = \bigoplus_i \Omega^1_{\Sigma/X,p_i}$.
On each summand, $\pi^*(1/x)$ differs from
$1/x(\pi(p_i))$ by an element of the maximal ideal acting
nilpotently on the finite-length module, giving a Jordan factor
$(1 - z/x(\pi(p_i)))^{\ell_i}$.
\end{proof}

\begin{definition}[Transport-compatible presentation]
\label{def:transport-compatible-presentation}
\index{transport!lifted cover}
A lifted presentation
$\cA \mapsto (L_\cA, y_\cA, r_\cA)$ is
\emph{transport-compatible} for an exact functor~$\Phi$ if for
every~$\cA$ there exists $u_{\Phi,\cA}(x,t) \in F(t)$ with
$y_{\Phi(\cA)} = u_{\Phi,\cA}(x, y_\cA) \in L_\cA$.
\end{definition}

\begin{theorem}[Transport of lifted covers; \ClaimStatusProvedHere]
\label{thm:transport-lifted-covers}
For a transport-compatible presentation, each~$\cA$ determines a
canonical finite dominant morphism
$f_{\Phi,\cA} \colon \Sigma_\cA \to \Sigma_{\Phi(\cA)}$
over~$X$. The relative cotangent complex
$\mathbb{D}_\Phi(\cA)
:= L_{\Sigma_\cA/\Sigma_{\Phi(\cA)}}$
measures the exact failure of branch-package preservation.
If $[L_\cA:F] = [L_{\Phi(\cA)}:F]$, then $f_{\Phi,\cA}$ is an
isomorphism and the full lifted branch package is preserved.
\end{theorem}

\begin{proof}
The rational transport law gives
$y_{\Phi(\cA)} \in F(y_\cA) = L_\cA$, hence
$L_{\Phi(\cA)} \subseteq L_\cA$. Normalization is
contravariantly functorial for finite field extensions, giving the
morphism. The relative cotangent complex captures the failure.
Equal degrees force the field inclusion to be an equality; a finite
birational morphism between normal curves is an isomorphism.
\end{proof}

\subsection{Quadratic sheet calculus}
\label{subsec:quadratic-sheet-calculus}
\index{quadratic spectral regime}

Let $L = F(u)$ with $u^2 = \Delta(x) \in F^\times$ and $\Delta$
not a square in~$F$. Every $y = a + bu \in L$ decomposes into an
even part~$a$ and an odd part~$bu$.

\begin{theorem}[Common-sheet multiplication law; \ClaimStatusProvedHere]
\label{thm:common-sheet-law}
For $y_1 = a_1 + b_1 u$ and $y_2 = a_2 + b_2 u$ on the same
quadratic sheet:
\[
y_1 y_2 = (a_1 a_2 + \Delta b_1 b_2)
 + (a_1 b_2 + a_2 b_1) u.
\]
In particular, $y_1 y_2 \in F$ if and only if
$a_1 b_2 + a_2 b_1 = 0$; if both factors are purely odd, then
$y_1 y_2 = \Delta b_1 b_2 \in F$.
\end{theorem}

\begin{proof}
Direct computation using $u^2 = \Delta$.
\end{proof}

\begin{remark}[What the visible discriminant misses]
\label{rem:visible-discriminant-misses}
The visible discriminant $\Delta(x) = u^2$ determines the sheet but
\emph{not} the odd component~$b$ of a lifted coordinate. Two
spectral objects with the same~$\Delta$ but different~$b$
have different tensor cancellation patterns: the product
$y_1 y_2$ depends on $b_1, b_2$, not just $\Delta$. The common
sheet is seen by the discriminant; the tensor arithmetic on that
sheet requires the lifted coordinate.
\end{remark}

For spectral objects on distinct quadratic sheets
$L_1 = F(u_1)$, $L_2 = F(u_2)$ with $u_1^2 = \Delta_1$,
$u_2^2 = \Delta_2$, the tensor compositum lives in
$L_1 L_2 = F(u_1, u_2)$ and carries a richer defect structure
measuring sheet loss and ramification.

\subsection{Cubic resolvent towers}
\label{subsec:cubic-resolvent-towers}
\index{cubic spectral regime}
\index{resolvent tower!cubic}
\index{Kummer theory!cubic}

In the cubic regime, $[L:F] = 3$ and the Galois closure has group
$S_3$. The resolvent tower has three layers: the odd resolvent
cover (quadratic), the even Kummer line, and the selector torsor.

\begin{definition}[Cubic spectral packet]
\label{def:cubic-spectral-packet}
\index{cubic spectral packet|textbf}
A \emph{cubic spectral packet} on $X$ consists of:
\begin{enumerate}[label=\textup{(\roman*)}]
\item a cubic field extension $L/F$ with
 $\operatorname{Gal}(\tilde{L}/F) \cong S_3$;
\item the quadratic resolvent field
 $K := \tilde{L}^{A_3} = F(\sqrt{D})$ where $D$ is the
 cubic discriminant;
\item the $K$-rational cube root $\omega$
 satisfying $\omega^3 = c \in K^\times$ via
 Cardano's formula;
\item the \emph{even Kummer line}:
 the class $[c] \in K^\times/(K^\times)^3$;
\item the \emph{selector torsor}: the choice of cube root
 among the three Galois conjugates.
\end{enumerate}
\end{definition}

\begin{theorem}[Hierarchy of spectral invariants; \ClaimStatusProvedHere]
\label{thm:spectral-hierarchy}
\index{spectral invariant!hierarchy}
For a spectral object with a chosen lifted presentation, the
following form a strict hierarchy:
\begin{enumerate}[label=\textup{(\roman*)}]
\item the \emph{visible shadow}: denominator/discriminant
 on~$X$;
\item the \emph{lifted cover}: the normalized finite cover
 $\Sigma \to X$;
\item \textup{(quadratic)} the \emph{odd component} of the
 lifted coordinate, controlling tensor cancellation;
\item \textup{(distinct quadratic)} the \emph{two-layer
 tensor defect} $(\delta_\otimes, \mathbb{D}_\otimes)$;
\item \textup{(cubic $S_3$)} the \emph{odd sheet}, the
 \emph{even Kummer line}, and the \emph{selector torsor}.
\end{enumerate}
Each successive layer contains information invisible to the
previous ones, and none of the later tensor- or
transport-theoretic formulas can be recovered from the visible
shadow alone.
\end{theorem}

\begin{proof}
The visible shadow is recovered from the branch operator by
Theorem~\ref{thm:determinantal-branch-formula} but does not
determine the odd part of a quadratic lifted coordinate
(Remark~\ref{rem:visible-discriminant-misses}). The common-sheet
multiplication law
(Theorem~\ref{thm:common-sheet-law}) shows that this odd part
controls quadratic tensor cancellation. In the distinct-sheet case,
sheet loss and ramified defect are different invariants. In the
cubic $S_3$ regime, the even Kummer line and selector torsor are
invisible to the odd resolvent cover.
\end{proof}

\begin{corollary}[The frontier is cubic; \ClaimStatusProvedHere]
\label{cor:frontier-is-cubic}
The quadratic tensor calculus is complete. Any genuinely new
phenomenon in the next rank must be cubic or higher. In the first
cubic case
\textup{(}the $\widehat{\mathfrak{sl}}_3 / \mathcal{W}_3$
orbit\textup{)}, the problem is to extract and compare the even
Kummer line and the selector torsor, not merely to detect the
visible odd sheet.
\end{corollary}

\begin{proof}
The preceding theorem shows that the rank-$1$ spectral cover
hierarchy is complete: the visible shadow, lifted coordinates,
tensor transport, DS reduction, and level--rank duality are all
determined by the quadratic discriminant calculus. Since no
additional quadratic invariants remain unaccounted for, any new
structural phenomenon must arise at cubic or higher order in the
spectral cover.
\end{proof}

\begin{remark}[Position in the shadow obstruction tower]
\label{rem:spectral-shadow-position}
\index{shadow tower!spectral level}
The lifted spectral cover sits at the $L_2$ level of the
characteristic hierarchy
(cf.~\S\ref{subsec:completion-kinematics-programme}): $L_1$ is the scalar
modular characteristic~$\kappa(\cA)$, while $L_2$ is the
spectral discriminant~$\Delta_\cA$ together with its module
structure. The defect calculus developed here provides the
geometric content of this level: the discriminant polynomial is
the visible shadow, but tensor transport, DS reduction, and
level-rank duality require the full lifted cover.
The $\widehat{\mathfrak{sl}}_3/\mathcal{W}_3$ orbit
(Conjectures~\ref{conj:sl3-bar-gf}
and~\ref{conj:w3-bar-gf}) is the first example where the
visible generating functions are both rational but the lifted
covers are nontrivially cubic: the algebra and its DS image
live on different sheets of a common $S_3$ cover, and the DS
functor is a sheet-crossing map.
\end{remark}

\begin{conjecture}[Universal bar growth rate $\gamma = 3$ for rank-$1$ Koszul algebras]
\label{conj:bar-growth-rate-rank-one}
\ClaimStatusConjectured
\index{bar complex!growth rate!rank-$1$ universality|textbf}
\index{bar cohomology!exponential growth rate}
For every rank-$1$ chirally Koszul algebra~$\cA$ with
interacting OPE \textup{(}i.e., excluding free fields\textup{)},
the bar cohomology generating function
$P_{\cA^!}(x) = \sum_{n \geq 1} \dim(\cA^!)_n\, x^n$
has dominant singularity at $x = 1/3$, giving exponential
growth rate
\begin{equation}\label{eq:bar-growth-rate-3}
 \gamma(\cA)
 \;:=\;
 \limsup_{n \to \infty}
 \bigl(\dim(\cA^!)_n\bigr)^{1/n}
 \;=\; 3.
\end{equation}
The value $\gamma = 3$ is \emph{universal} across rank-$1$
families: Virasoro \textup{(}Motzkin differences,
$P(x) = (1 - x - \sqrt{1{-}2x{-}3x^2})/(2x)$,
degree-$2$ algebraic\textup{)},
$\beta\gamma$ \textup{(}$P(x) = \sqrt{(1{+}x)/(1{-}3x)}$,
degree-$2$ algebraic\textup{)},
$bc$ system \textup{(}$P(x) = (2{-}3x)/(1{-}3x)$,
rational\textup{)}.
All three have the same exponential rate despite distinct
algebraic structures and distinct shadow depth classes
\textup{(}$\mathbf{M}$, $\mathbf{C}$, $\mathbf{L}$
respectively\textup{)}.

The conjecture asserts that $\gamma = 3$ is an invariant of
the rank, not of the individual algebra: every rank-$1$
interacting Koszul algebra has the same bar-cohomology
exponential growth rate.
\end{conjecture}

\begin{remark}[Evidence]
\label{rem:bar-growth-rate-evidence}
Verified for all rank-$1$ families in
Table~\textup{\ref{tab:bar-dimensions}}
by the compute engine
\texttt{bar\_gf\_universality\_engine} (104~tests).
The common value $\gamma = 3$ arises because the dominant
singularity at $x = 1/3$ is a zero of the shared
discriminant factor $(1 - 3x)$: for rank-$1$ algebras,
the spectral discriminant always contains $(1 - 3x)$ as a
factor
\textup{(}Theorem~\textup{\ref{thm:ds-bar-gf-discriminant}}).
The free-field exception \textup{(}Heisenberg, free
fermion\textup{)} has sub-exponential growth
\textup{(}$\gamma = 1$\textup{)},
consistent with their transcendental partition-type
generating functions
\textup{(}Corollary~\textup{\ref{cor:subexp-free-field}}).
For $\widehat{\mathfrak{sl}}_2$, the growth is merely
linear \textup{(}$\dim H^n = 2n{+}1$,
$\gamma = 1$\textup{)}: this is the unique rank-$1$
interacting algebra with sub-exponential bar growth, by
Garland--Lepowsky concentration
\textup{(}Remark~\textup{\ref{rem:garland-lepowsky-sl2}}).
The conjecture therefore applies to rank-$1$ algebras
with \emph{non-semisimple} negative part; the semisimple
case $\widehat{\mathfrak{sl}}_2$ is excluded by the
stronger Garland--Lepowsky mechanism.
\end{remark}

\begin{conjecture}[Rank-$2$ bar generating function rationality with
golden-ratio denominator]
\label{conj:rank-two-golden-ratio-denominator}
\ClaimStatusConjectured
\index{bar complex!generating function!rank-$2$ rationality|textbf}
\index{golden ratio!bar generating function denominator}
\index{bar cohomology!rank-$2$ pattern}
For rank-$2$ chirally Koszul algebras, the bar cohomology
generating function is rational \textup{(}not merely
algebraic\textup{)}, and its denominator contains the
irreducible quadratic factor
\begin{equation}\label{eq:golden-ratio-denominator}
 1 - 3x - x^2,
\end{equation}
whose roots are $x = (-3 \pm \sqrt{13})/2$. The dominant
root $x_0 = (-3 + \sqrt{13})/2 \approx 0.303$ gives the
exponential growth rate
\begin{equation}\label{eq:rank-two-growth-rate}
 \gamma_2
 \;=\;
 1/x_0
 \;=\;
 \frac{3 + \sqrt{13}}{2}
 \;\approx\; 3.303.
\end{equation}
The conjecture is supported by the two computed rank-$2$
families:
\begin{enumerate}[label=\textup{(\roman*)}]
\item $\widehat{\mathfrak{sl}}_3$:
 $P(x) = 4x(2 - 13x - 2x^2)/((1 - 8x)(1 - 3x - x^2))$
 \textup{(}Conjecture~\textup{\ref{conj:sl3-bar-gf}}).

\item $\mathcal{W}_3$:
 $P(x) = x(2 - 3x)/((1 - x)(1 - 3x - x^2))$
 \textup{(}Conjecture~\textup{\ref{conj:w3-bar-gf}}).
\end{enumerate}
Both share the factor $(1 - 3x - x^2)$, which is the
DS-invariant discriminant subfactor
\textup{(}Proposition~\textup{\ref{prop:ds-invariant-discriminant}}).
The pattern predicts that \emph{every} rank-$2$ Koszul
algebra, including non-simply-laced types
$\widehat{B}_2$, $\widehat{G}_2$, and their DS images,
has a rational bar GF with $(1 - 3x - x^2)$ in the
denominator.
\end{conjecture}

\begin{remark}[Evidence]
\label{rem:rank-two-golden-evidence}
The generating functions in~(i) and~(ii) are conjectural
\textup{(}Conjectures~\textup{\ref{conj:sl3-bar-gf}}
and~\textup{\ref{conj:w3-bar-gf}}), but the shared
quadratic factor $(1 - 3x - x^2)$ is proved
\textup{(}Proposition~\textup{\ref{prop:ds-invariant-discriminant}}).
The rationality
\textup{(}as opposed to the degree-$2$ algebraicity of
rank-$1$\textup{)} is conjectured from the rank-plus-one
pattern
\textup{(}Remark~\textup{\ref{rem:rank-plus-one}}):
rank-$r$ bar GFs should satisfy a holonomic
recurrence of order $r + 1$, and for $r \geq 2$ the
solution space of a third-order (or higher) Fuchsian ODE
generically contains only rational functions.
Computational support from the engines
\texttt{bar\_gf\_universality\_engine} (104~tests) and
\texttt{bar\_cohomology\_sl3\_explicit\_engine}.
\end{remark}

\section{A conjectural period map for the shadow tower}

The scalar lane isolates $\kappa(\cA)$, but it does not decide
which higher shadows can occur together. The missing object is a
period domain for the characteristic projections of
$\Theta_\cA$. Fix a smooth projective curve~$X$, a connected
level stratum inside one family, a genus window
$0 \le g \le g_0$, and a degree cutoff $N \ge 4$.
For a deformation point represented by an MC element~$\Theta$
in the local moduli space $\mathcal{M}^{\mathrm{mod}}_\cA$
of Proposition~\ref{prop:modular-homotopy-type-structure}, write
\[
V^{\mathrm{per}}_{\le N,g_0}(\cA)
:=
\bigoplus_{r=2}^{N}\;\bigoplus_{g=0}^{g_0}
\cA^{\mathrm{sh}}_{r,g},
\qquad
\operatorname{Sh}_{r,\le g_0}(\Theta)
:=
\{\pi_{r,g}(\Theta)\}_{0 \le g \le g_0}.
\]
The truncated period vector is
\[
\operatorname{Per}_{\le N,g_0}(\Theta)
:=
\bigl(
\kappa(\Theta),\,
[\mathfrak{C}(\Theta)],\,
[\mathfrak{Q}(\Theta)],\,
\operatorname{Sh}_{5,\le g_0}(\Theta),\ldots,
\operatorname{Sh}_{N,\le g_0}(\Theta)
\bigr),
\]
with $\kappa(\Theta)$ the scalar shadow of
Construction~\ref{constr:shadow-extraction-explicit},
$[\mathfrak{C}(\Theta)]$ the cubic class, and
$[\mathfrak{Q}(\Theta)]$ the quartic class. Its shadow flag is
\[
F^p_\Theta
:=
\bigl\langle
\operatorname{Sh}_{r,\le g_0}(\Theta)
\;:\;
p \le r \le N
\bigr\rangle,
\qquad
2 \le p \le N.
\]
This packages characteristic data extracted from $\Theta_\cA$.
It is not an identification of $B(\cA)$, $\cA^!$, or
$Z^{\mathrm{der}}_{\mathrm{ch}}(\cA)$.

\begin{conjecture}[Period map for the shadow flag]
\label{conj:theta-period-map}
\ClaimStatusConjectured
For every fixed scope $(X,g_0,N)$ and every connected level
stratum inside one family, there exists a period domain
\[
\mathcal{D}^{\mathrm{sh}}_{\le N,g_0}(\cA)
\subseteq
\operatorname{Flag}\!\bigl(V^{\mathrm{per}}_{\le N,g_0}(\cA)\bigr)
\]
and a local period map
\[
\Phi^{\mathrm{sh}}_{\le N,g_0,\cA}
\colon
(\mathcal{M}^{\mathrm{mod}}_\cA,\Theta_\cA)
\longrightarrow
\mathcal{D}^{\mathrm{sh}}_{\le N,g_0}(\cA)
\]
whose value at a deformation point~$\Theta$ is the shadow flag
$F^\bullet_\Theta$. The domain is cut out by the projected
Maurer--Cartan equations, the clutching compatibilities along the
boundary strata of $\overline{\mathcal{M}}_{g,r}$, and the
complementarity constraints already satisfied by the shadow tower.
As $g_0$ and~$N$ increase, these maps are compatible and assemble
to a pro-period map for the full tower~$\Theta_\cA$.
\end{conjecture}

\begin{conjecture}[Shadow Griffiths transversality]
\label{conj:theta-griffiths-transversality}
\ClaimStatusConjectured
On the period domain of
Conjecture~\ref{conj:theta-period-map}, the twisted differential
$d_\Theta = D + [\Theta,-]$ of
Proposition~\ref{prop:saddle-point-mc} induces, after projection to
the shadow flag, a one-step shadow differential
\[
D_{\mathrm{sh}}
\colon
F^p_\Theta/F^{p+1}_\Theta
\longrightarrow
F^{p+1}_\Theta/F^{p+2}_\Theta,
\qquad
2 \le p \le N-1.
\]
Equivalently, an infinitesimal deformation may move shadow grade~$p$
only into grade~$p{+}1$. Hence the derivative of the period map
lands in a proper horizontal subbundle
\[
d\Phi^{\mathrm{sh}}_{\le N,g_0,\cA}
\bigl(
T_\Theta \mathcal{M}^{\mathrm{mod}}_\cA
\bigr)
\subseteq
T^{\mathrm{hor}}
\mathcal{D}^{\mathrm{sh}}_{\le N,g_0}(\cA)
\subsetneq
T\mathcal{D}^{\mathrm{sh}}_{\le N,g_0}(\cA).
\]
In particular, a sequence of shadows occurs as a period vector
only if it satisfies the one-step transport relations obtained by
projecting the Maurer--Cartan equation grade by grade.
\end{conjecture}

\begin{conjecture}[The depth classes as period-domain strata]
\label{conj:theta-depth-period-strata}
\ClaimStatusConjectured
The four shadow-depth classes
$\mathbf{G}/\mathbf{L}/\mathbf{C}/\mathbf{M}$ are recovered from the
topology of the shadow period domain:
\begin{enumerate}[label=\textup{(\roman*)}]
\item \textup{$\mathbf{G}$, depth $r = 2$.}
 The image of the period map is discrete: the smallest horizontal
 stratum through the image is $0$-dimensional.
\item \textup{$\mathbf{L}$, depth $r = 3$.}
 The image lies on curve-like horizontal strata, with one cubic
 direction and no quartic thickening.
\item \textup{$\mathbf{C}$, depth $r = 4$.}
 The image lies on surface-like strata: the quartic period
 direction is nonzero, but higher horizontal transport is blocked
 by stratum separation.
\item \textup{$\mathbf{M}$, depth $r = \infty$.}
 The image meets a dense open set on which, for every finite
 cutoff~$N$, the period map has maximal rank compatible with the
 horizontal bundle.
\end{enumerate}
Thus shadow depth is the local topological dimension of the smallest
horizontal stratum carrying the period image, rather than a scalar
invariant seen at degree~$2$ alone.
\end{conjecture}

\begin{conjecture}[Discriminant divisor and Koszul factorization]
\label{conj:theta-koszul-period-factorization}
\ClaimStatusConjectured
Let
\[
\Delta(\Theta)
\;:=\;
8\,\kappa(\Theta)\,S_4(\Theta)
\]
be the critical discriminant of
Theorem~\ref{thm:single-line-dichotomy} in the fixed family-level
stratum. Then $\Delta = 0$ is the primary discriminant divisor in
the shadow period domain
$\mathcal{D}^{\mathrm{sh}}_{\le N,g_0}(\cA)$: its complement is the
open $\mathbf{M}$-region, while its internal stratification separates
the finite-depth classes $\mathbf{G}$, $\mathbf{L}$, and
$\mathbf{C}$.

If
$\mathcal{M}^{\mathrm{Kos}}_\cA \subset \mathcal{M}^{\mathrm{mod}}_\cA$
denotes the local Koszul locus of
Theorem~\ref{thm:higher-genus-inversion}, then the period map
factors through a proper subdomain
\[
\Phi^{\mathrm{sh}}_{\le N,g_0,\cA}\big|_{\mathcal{M}^{\mathrm{Kos}}_\cA}
\colon
(\mathcal{M}^{\mathrm{Kos}}_\cA,\Theta_\cA)
\longrightarrow
\mathcal{D}^{\mathrm{Kos}}_{\le N,g_0}(\cA)
\subsetneq
\mathcal{D}^{\mathrm{sh}}_{\le N,g_0}(\cA),
\]
where $\mathcal{D}^{\mathrm{Kos}}_{\le N,g_0}(\cA)$ is cut out by the
Riccati algebraicity relations and complementarity identities
already satisfied on the Koszul locus. In this smaller domain the
higher period coordinates satisfy universal algebraic constraints over
the low-shadow data
$\bigl(\kappa(\Theta),[\mathfrak{C}(\Theta)],
[\mathfrak{Q}(\Theta)]\bigr)$.
\end{conjecture}

\begin{remark}[Evidence]
\label{rem:theta-period-map-evidence}
Construction~\ref{constr:shadow-extraction-explicit} already gives
the characteristic coordinates entering the proposed period vector,
and Proposition~\ref{prop:modular-homotopy-type-structure} gives the
formal moduli space on which the period map should live.
Construction~\ref{constr:primitive-flat-connection} and
Proposition~\ref{prop:saddle-point-mc} supply the twisted
differential that suggests the one-step shadow transport of
Conjecture~\ref{conj:theta-griffiths-transversality}. The topology of
the four classes is already visible in
Theorem~\ref{thm:single-line-dichotomy},
Remark~\ref{rem:affine-shadow-metric},
Remark~\ref{rem:contact-stratum-separation}, and
Theorem~\ref{thm:genus2-shell-activation}: class~$\mathbf{G}$ has no
higher direction, class~$\mathbf{L}$ has only the cubic lane,
class~$\mathbf{C}$ has a quartic surface direction but no infinite
continuation, and class~$\mathbf{M}$ has non-terminating higher
transport. The critical discriminant
$\Delta = 8\kappa(\Theta)S_4(\Theta)$ already separates the open
$\mathbf{M}$-region from the primary divisor; what is missing is its period-domain
interpretation.
\end{remark}

\begin{remark}[Period-map interpretation of the shadow tower]
\label{rem:period-map-theta}
\ClaimStatusHeuristic
The conjectural period map
$\Phi^{\mathrm{sh}}_{\le N,g_0,\cA}$ of
Conjecture~\ref{conj:theta-period-map} admits the following
heuristic reading. The Maurer--Cartan element
$\Theta_\cA = D_\cA - d_0$, satisfying
$d\,\Theta + \tfrac{1}{2}[\Theta,\Theta] = 0$, assigns to each
point of the moduli space
$\mathcal{M}^{\mathrm{mod}}_\cA$ a \emph{period point} in the
shadow flag variety
$\operatorname{Flag}(V^{\mathrm{per}}_{\le N,g_0}(\cA))$.
The shadow projections
$\kappa(\Theta)$, $[\mathfrak{C}(\Theta)]$,
$[\mathfrak{Q}(\Theta)]$ serve as coordinate functions on
this flag variety: $\kappa$ is the scalar coordinate (degree~$2$),
the cubic class $\mathfrak{C}$ opens the first higher direction,
and the quartic class $\mathfrak{Q}$ opens the second.

Under this reading, the
$\mathbf{G}/\mathbf{L}/\mathbf{C}/\mathbf{M}$ classification
(Theorem~\ref{thm:single-line-dichotomy}) describes the
topology of the period image:
\begin{itemize}
\item Class~$\mathbf{G}$ (Heisenberg, depth $r=2$): the period
 image is a single point; $\kappa$ is the only nonvanishing
 coordinate and the shadow tower terminates at degree~$2$.
\item Class~$\mathbf{L}$ (affine Kac--Moody, depth $r=3$): the
 image traces a curve in the flag variety, parametrized by the
 cubic direction $\mathfrak{C}$; no quartic thickening.
\item Class~$\mathbf{C}$ ($\beta\gamma_\lambda$, depth $r=4$): the image
 is a surface, with the quartic coordinate $\mathfrak{Q}$
 providing the second independent direction; higher transport
 is blocked by stratum separation.
\item Class~$\mathbf{M}$ (Virasoro and $\mathcal{W}_N$, depth
 $r=\infty$): the image meets a dense open subset of the
 period domain; for every finite cutoff~$N$ the period map has
 maximal horizontal rank.
\end{itemize}
The discriminant $\Delta = 8\kappa\, S_4$ of
Theorem~\ref{thm:single-line-dichotomy} is the equation of the
primary divisor separating the finite-dimensional period domains
(classes~$\mathbf{G}$ and~$\mathbf{L}$, where $\Delta = 0$) from
the infinite-tower region (class~$\mathbf{M}$, where
$\Delta \neq 0$). Theorem~D
(Theorem~\ref{thm:genus-universality}), asserting
$\mathrm{obs}_g(\cA) = \kappa(\cA)\,\lambda_g$ in the
uniform-weight setting , states that the
universal Hodge class $\lambda_g$ on
$\overline{\mathcal{M}}_g$ pulls back along the period map to
the genus-$g$ obstruction: the arithmetic of $\lambda_g$ on
moduli space is the shadow of the scalar period
coordinate~$\kappa$.

The classical analogue is the Torelli period map
$\tau_g\colon \mathcal{M}_g \hookrightarrow \mathcal{A}_g
= \mathfrak{h}_g / \mathrm{Sp}_{2g}(\mathbb{Z})$,
which injects the moduli of genus-$g$ curves into the moduli of
principally polarized abelian $g$-folds (Siegel upper
half-space). Torelli's theorem asserts that $\tau_g$ is
injective: a smooth curve is determined by its Jacobian.
Griffiths transversality constrains the infinitesimal variation
of Hodge structure along the image, and the Schottky problem
asks for the intrinsic characterization of the Jacobian
locus~$\tau_g(\mathcal{M}_g) \subset \mathcal{A}_g$. In genus
$g \le 3$, every principally polarized abelian variety is a
Jacobian and the Schottky problem is vacuous; in genus~$g \ge 4$
the Jacobian locus is a proper subvariety defined by
transcendental constraints (the Schottky--Jung relations and
their descendants).

The shadow period map replaces each term in this classical
picture. The source is not the moduli of curves but the Koszul
moduli space $\mathcal{M}^{\mathrm{mod}}_\cA$, parametrizing
chiral algebras up to Koszul equivalence. The target is not
the Siegel domain but the shadow flag variety
$\operatorname{Flag}(V^{\mathrm{per}}_{\le N,g_0}(\cA))$, whose
successive graded pieces are indexed by the degree filtration.
The conjectural shadow Torelli statement is the injectivity of
$\Phi^{\mathrm{sh}}_{\le N,g_0,\cA}$: a chiral algebra should
be determined, up to Koszul equivalence, by its shadow data
$(\kappa, \mathfrak{C}, \mathfrak{Q}, \ldots)$, just as a curve
is determined by its periods. The transversality constraint
(Conjecture~\ref{conj:theta-griffiths-transversality})
is the one-step shadow transport
$D_{\mathrm{sh}}\colon F^p/F^{p+1} \to F^{p+1}/F^{p+2}$
imposed by the projected Maurer--Cartan equation: the shadow
period point cannot move arbitrarily in the flag variety but
must advance one filtration step at a time, exactly as
the classical period map respects the Hodge filtration.

The $\mathbf{G}/\mathbf{L}/\mathbf{C}/\mathbf{M}$ classification
is the shadow Schottky problem. The Schottky problem stratifies
$\mathcal{A}_g$ by asking which abelian varieties lie in the
Jacobian locus; the depth classification stratifies the shadow
flag variety by the dimension of the period image.
Class~$\mathbf{G}$ corresponds to genus $\le 1$ of the classical
problem, where the period map is trivially surjective onto a
point; class~$\mathbf{L}$ to genus~$2$, where the image is a
curve and the Schottky constraint is a single equation;
class~$\mathbf{C}$ to genus~$3$, where the image is a surface
and one further relation (stratum separation, the analogue of the
Schottky--Jung relation) closes the system; and class~$\mathbf{M}$
to genus~$\ge 4$, where the image has maximal horizontal rank
and no finite set of polynomial constraints suffices. The
decisive advantage of the shadow setting is that the discriminant
$\Delta = 8\kappa\, S_4$ resolves the stratification completely:
the shadow Schottky problem reduces to a single algebraic
condition ($\Delta = 0$ versus $\Delta \neq 0$), whereas the
classical problem remains transcendental. This is inevitable:
the shadow flag variety at each finite degree truncation is
finite-dimensional and algebraic, so the ``Schottky locus''
(the image of the period map) is cut out by polynomial
equations in finitely many shadow coordinates.
\end{remark}
